title: Te Arotake Hōtaka: Assessing the Mangakōtukutuku Stream Restoration Project | Te Arotake Hōtaka: Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Technology
culturalContext: Students will investigate the ongoing restoration of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a culturally significant stream that flows through our rohe. This awa is mentioned in Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau and was traditionally a mahinga kai site for tuna (eels) and kōura (crayfish). The project connects to the work of local kaumātua Hōri Waititi, who has led community efforts to restore the mauri of this waterway.
learningObjectives: Analyze the environmental impact of the stream restoration using digital mapping tools to identify at least three positive ecological changes
Design and create a simple water quality testing device using locally sourced materials and traditional knowledge principles
Develop a digital presentation that incorporates mātauranga Māori perspectives on water conservation alongside technological solutions
Collaborate with local experts to assess the cultural and environmental outcomes of the restoration project

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki te Awa
activities.description: Visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with kaumātua Hōri Waititi to hear pūrākau about the stream, learn traditional monitoring methods, and understand the cultural significance of this waterway to Ngāti Kahungunu.

activities.title: Hōtaka Aroturuki
activities.description: Use digital mapping tools (Google Earth, GIS) and water testing kits to document current water quality, identify native species returning to the area, and map the restoration progress compared to historical data.

activities.title: Hangahanga Taonga
activities.description: Design and build simple water monitoring devices using natural materials (flax, wood) and basic sensors, incorporating traditional patterns and modern technology to create functional assessment tools.

activities.title: Whiriwhiri Kōrero
activities.description: Interview project leaders from the regional council and mana whenua representatives to understand different perspectives on the technological and cultural approaches used in the restoration.

activities.title: Whakatauira Matihiko
activities.description: Create digital presentations using TinkerCAD or similar software to propose additional technological solutions that could enhance the restoration while honoring Māori environmental values.

resources: Kaumātua Hōri Waititi (local cultural expert and restoration leader)
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku Restoration Project' reports and data
NIWA's freshwater monitoring guides and digital mapping resources
Traditional Māori water conservation knowledge from 'Te Māra o Tāne' resource book

tasks: Create a comprehensive digital impact assessment report comparing traditional and technological monitoring methods
Design and present a prototype water quality testing device that blends mātauranga Māori with modern technology
Develop a culturally-responsive proposal for the next phase of stream restoration using appropriate technologies

nzcAlignment: Technological Practice: Planning for practice, brief development, outcome development and evaluation
Nature of Technology: Characteristics of technology, characteristics of technological outcomes
Technology and Society: Current and future issues, historical influences
Key Competencies: Participating and contributing, relating to others
